title: Gold Hair - A Story Of Pornic
content:
I.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, the beautiful girl, too white,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who lived at Pornic, down by the sea,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just where the sea and the Loire unite!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a boasted name in Brittany
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She bore, which I will not write.

II.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Too white, for the flower of life is red;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her flesh was the soft seraphic screen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of a soul that is meant (her parents said)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To just see earth, and hardly be seen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And blossom in heaven instead.

III.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet earth saw one thing, one how fair!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One grace that grew to its full on earth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Smiles might be sparse on her cheek so spare,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And her waist want half a girdleן¿½s girth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But she had her great gold hair.

IV.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hair, such a wonder of flix and floss,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Freshness and fragrance, floods of it, too!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gold, did I say? Nay, goldן¿½s mere dross:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here, Life smiled, ן¿½Think what I meant to do!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Love sighed, ן¿½Fancy my loss!ן¿½

V.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So, when she died, it was scarce more strange
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than that, when delicate evening dies,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And you follow its spent sunן¿½s pallid range,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s a shoot of colour startles the skies
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With sudden, violent change,

VI.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That, while the breath was nearly to seek,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As they put the little cross to her lips,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She changed; a spot came out on her cheek,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A spark from her eye in mid-eclipse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And she broke forth, ן¿½I must speak!ן¿½

VII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Not my hair!ן¿½ made the girl her moan,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½All the rest is gone or to go;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But the last, last grace, my all, my own,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Let it stay in the grave, that the ghosts may know!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Leave my poor gold hair alone!ן¿½

VIII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The passion thus vented, dead lay she;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her parents sobbed their worst on that;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All friends joined in, nor observed degree
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For indeed the hair was to wonder at,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As it spread, not flowing free,

IX.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But curled around her brow, like a crown,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And coiled beside her cheeks, like a cap,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And calmed about her neck, ay, down
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To her breast, pressed flat, without a gap
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ the gold, it reached her gown.

X.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All kissed that face, like a silver wedge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mid the yellow wealth, nor disturbed its hair
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Eן¿½en the priest allowed deathן¿½s privilege,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As he planted the crucifix with care
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On her breast, ן¿½twixt edge and edge.

XI.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thus was she buried, inviolate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of body and soul, in the very space
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the altar; keeping saintly state
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Pornic church, for her pride of race,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pure life and piteous fate.

XII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in after-time would your fresh tear fall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though your mouth might twitch with a dubious smile,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As they told you of gold, both robe and pall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How she prayed them leave it alone awhile,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So it never was touched at all.

XIII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Years flew; this legend grew at last
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The life of the lady; all she had done,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All been, in the memories fading fast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of lover and friend, was summed in one
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sentence survivors passed:

XIV.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To wit, she was meant for heaven, not earth;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had turned an angel before the time:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet, since she was mortal, in such dearth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of frailty, all you could count a crime
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was, she knew her gold hairן¿½s worth.

XV.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At little pleasant Pornic church,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It chanced, the pavement wanted repair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was taken to pieces: left in the lurch,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A certain sacred space lay bare,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the boys began research.

XVI.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½T was the space where our sires would lay a saint,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A benefactor, a bishop, suppose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A baron with armour-adornments quaint,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dame with chased ring and jewelled rose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Things sanctity saves from taint;

XVII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So we come to find them in after-days
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the corpse is presumed to have done with gauds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of use to the living, in many ways
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the boys get pelf, and the town applauds,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the church deserves the praise.

XVIII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They grubbed with a will: and at length, O cor
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Humanum, pectora cן¿½ca, and the rest!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They found no gaud they were prying for,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No ring, no rose, but who would have guessed?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A double Louis-dן¿½or!

XIX.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here was a case for the priest: he heard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Marked, inwardly digested, laid
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Finger on nose, smiled, ן¿½Thereן¿½s a bird
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Chirps in my earן¿½: then, ן¿½Bring a spade,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dig deeper!ן¿½ he gave the word.

XX.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And lo, when they came to the coffin-lid,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or rotten planks which composed it once,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why, there lay the girlן¿½s skull wedged amid
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A mint of money, it served for the nonce
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To hold in its hair-heaps hid!

XXI.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hid there? Why? Could the girl be wont
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (She the stainless soul) to treasure up
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Money, earthן¿½s trash and heavenן¿½s affront?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had a spider found out the communion-cup,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was a toad in the christening-font?

XXII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Truth is truth: too true it was.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gold! She hoarded and hugged it first,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Longed for it, leaned oן¿½er it, loved it, alas,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till the humour grew to a head and burst,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And she cried, at the final pass,

XXIII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Talk not of God, my heart is stone!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Nor lover nor friend, be gold for both!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Gold I lack; and, my all, my own,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½It shall hide in my hair. I scarce die loth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½If they let my hair alone!ן¿½

XXIV.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Louis-dן¿½or, some six times five,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And duly double, every piece.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now do you see? With the priest to shrive,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With parents preventing her soulן¿½s release
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By kisses that kept alive,

XXV.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With heavenן¿½s gold gates about to ope,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With friendsן¿½ praise, gold-like, lingering still,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ An instinct had bidden the girlן¿½s hand grope
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For gold, the true sort, ן¿½Gold in heaven, if you will;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But I keep earthן¿½s too, I hope.ן¿½

XXVI.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Enough! The priest took the graveן¿½s grim yield
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The parents, they eyed that price of sin
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As if thirty pieces lay revealed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the place to bury strangers in,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The hideous Potterן¿½s Field.

XXVII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the priest bethought him: ן¿½ן¿½Milk thatן¿½s spiltן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You know the adage! Watch and pray!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Saints tumble to earth with so slight a tilt!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½It would build a new altar; that, we may!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the altar therewith was built.

XXVIII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why I deliver this horrible verse?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As the text of a sermon, which now I preach:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Evil or good may be better or worse
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the human heart, but the mixture of each
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is a marvel and a curse.

XXIX.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The candid incline to surmise of late
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That the Christian faith proves false, I find:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For our Essays-and-Reviewsן¿½ debate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Begins to tell on the public mind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Colensoן¿½s words have weight:

XXX.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I still, to suppose it true, for my part,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ See reasons and reasons; this, to begin:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½T is the faith that launched point-blank her dart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the head of a lie, taught Original Sin.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Corruption of Manן¿½s Heart.
meaning: About: Robert Browning was a British poet and playwright whose mastery of dramatic verse, especially dramatic monologues, made him one of the foremost Victorian poets.
